lang.selector.label: Language:
update.dialog.title: Update Available
update.dialog.heading: 🎉 Pentra {new_version} is available
update.dialog.body_html:
A new version of Pentra has been released.

Current: {current_version}
Latest: {new_version}

Click Open Download Page to get the latest release from GitHub.
auth.title: Authorization
auth.subtitle: Please read and accept the terms of use before continuing.
auth.intro_html:
Pentra is designed to be used only on systems you own or for which you have written authorization.

Unauthorized scanning may constitute a criminal offense under applicable laws and can result in imprisonment.
auth.features_html:
What Pentra does:

  • Detects open ports and services
  • Reports known security issues
  • Provides step-by-step remediation guidance
  • Saves the report only to your desktop — never uploads it online

What Pentra does NOT do:

  • Does not run attacks or exploits — detection only
  • Does not crack passwords, send malicious payloads, or erase traces
  • Does not send your data to a remote server
auth.checkbox_owner: I declare that I own the systems I will scan, or that I have written authorization to scan them.
auth.checkbox_terms: I have read and accept Pentra's terms of use (detection only, reports are saved locally).
target.title: Target Selection
target.subtitle: Which system would you like to scan?
target.info_html: Pentra supports multiple target types. Pick one of the active options below and fill in the required fields.
target.localhost.label: 🖥️  This computer (localhost — 127.0.0.1)
target.localhost.desc_html: <small>Scans the open ports and services on your own machine. The safest option — packets are sent only to your own device.</small>
target.url.label: 🔗  Website (URL)
target.url.desc_html: On the URL you specify, checks security headers, SSL/TLS vulnerabilities, exposed sensitive files, path traversal, SQL injection and XSS. Only sites you own or have written authorization for.
target.url.checkbox: I own this URL or have written authorization (required for public/external targets)
target.wifi.label: 📡  Nearby Wi-Fi networks
target.wifi.desc_html: <small>Passively lists nearby Wi-Fi networks — no packets are sent. Detects weakly-encrypted (WEP, open) or outdated networks.</small>
target.local_net.label: 🏠  My local network (auto-detect)
target.local_net.desc_html: <small>Discovers all devices on your home/office network. Detects the subnet you're connected to automatically (usually /24 = 254 hosts).</small>
target.local_net.detected_html: Detected: {cidr} — devices on this network will be scanned (~254 hosts).
target.local_net.not_detected_html: <small style='color: #d32f2f;'>Local network could not be detected — make sure the computer is connected to a network or use the 'IP range' option.</small>
target.ip_range.label: 🌐  Specific IP or IP range (CIDR)
target.ip_range.checkbox: I own this range or have written authorization (required for external IPs)
target.dialog.local_not_detected.title: Local network not detected
target.dialog.local_not_detected.body: Make sure the computer is connected to a network, or use the 'IP range' option to enter a CIDR manually.
target.dialog.invalid_cidr.title: Invalid CIDR
target.dialog.invalid_cidr.body: Please enter a valid IP or CIDR format (e.g. 192.168.1.0/24, 10.0.0.1).
target.dialog.invalid_url.title: Invalid URL
target.dialog.invalid_url.body: Please enter a valid URL starting with http:// or https://.
depth.title: Scan Depth
depth.subtitle: How deep should it go? Deeper = longer, but more comprehensive.
depth.quick.label: 🟢  Quick  —  about 1-2 min
depth.quick.desc_html:
Does: Scans the 100 most common ports, detects open ports and the name of the service running.
Does not: Service version, vulnerability scan, OS detection.
Ideal for a general health check.
depth.standard.label: 🟡  Standard  —  about 5-15 min
depth.standard.desc_html:
Does: Top 1000 ports + full service version detection + known CVE matching (from the NVD database).
Does not: Deep exploit scripts, OS detection.
Recommended: ideal for a real-system audit.
depth.deep.label: 🔴  Deep  —  about 30-60 min
depth.deep.desc_html:
Does: All 65,535 ports + service version + OS detection + safe NSE scripts (common vulnerabilities and discovery) + known CVE matching.
Does not: Exploit execution (never, in any version).
Tip: OS detection works best when Pentra is launched as administrator (Npcap required).
progress.title: Scan In Progress
progress.subtitle: Please wait — this may take a few minutes.
report.title: Scan Report
report.subtitle: Findings are listed below. Use the button to save the report to your desktop.
report.risk.header_html:
OVERALL RISK
{score}/10  {label}
report.risk.vs_previous_html:
VS PREVIOUS SCAN
{trend} ({delta})
report.empty_html:
✅ No findings were detected.

No visible issues were found at the depth you selected. The Standard/Deep option will be available in a future release for more comprehensive scanning.
report.dialog.save.title: Save Report
report.dialog.save_failed.title: Save Failed
risk.summary.empty: No security issues were detected at the scan depth you selected. Your system appears clean within the scope of this scan — well done. For a more comprehensive scan, try the Standard/Deep option.
risk.summary.sentence: Your system has {count} finding(s) at the levels: {parts}. Risk level: {label} ({score}/10). {tone}
risk.summary.tone_urgent: Immediate action required — some findings could allow an attacker to damage your system.
risk.summary.tone_moderate: Your system is not in an overall dangerous state, but certain configuration improvements are important.
risk.summary.tone_low: Low-priority improvements — no urgent risk, but recommended for best practices.
report.tpl.subtitle: Individual security audit report — {date}
report.tpl.findings.cve_lang_note: ℹ️ Descriptions are from NVD's official English text — the security community's common language.
report.tpl.findings.guide.toggle: 📘 Detailed Remediation Guide — click to expand
report.tpl.findings.empty_desc: No visible security issues were found at the scan depth you selected. For a more comprehensive scan, try Standard or Deep mode.
report.tpl.footer.generated: Pentra — Local security scanner · Report generated on {date}
report.tpl.footer.warning: ⚠️ This report may contain sensitive information. Store it in a safe place.
probe.web.security_headers.description: Detects missing security settings in HTTP response headers
probe.web.exposed_paths.description: Public access check for sensitive files/folders
progress.network.no_response: Target did not respond — firewall may be closed or device unreachable
progress.network.time_estimate: Network {network} ({host_count} addresses) is being scanned at {depth_text} depth. Live devices are detected first, then their ports. Estimated time: ~{minutes_est}. The sub-log is not updated while Nmap runs — please be patient.
error.network.nmap_missing: python-nmap library is not installed: {error}. Install it via pip install python-nmap.
error.network.nmap_not_found: Nmap is not installed or was not found: {error}. Install from https://nmap.org/download.html.
finding.network.open_port.title: Open port: {port}/{proto} ({service})
finding.network.open_port.desc: TCP port {port} is open on {host}. Service name: {service}.{version_part}{extra_note}
finding.network.open_port.remediation: If this port is not truly needed, close it. If it is needed, restrict access via firewall to trusted IP ranges only and ensure the service software is up to date.
finding.network.generic_port.remediation: Close the port if not needed. Restrict the firewall to allow only specific IPs.
note.network.port.ftp: FTP — unencrypted file transfer, may allow anonymous access
note.network.port.telnet: Telnet — password sent in clear text, use SSH instead
note.network.port.smtp: SMTP — may be an open relay if misconfigured
note.network.port.rpc: Windows RPC — should be closed to the outside
note.network.port.mssql: MSSQL — increases attack surface if exposed externally
note.network.port.rdp: RDP — common brute-force target, strong password + MFA required
note.network.port.vnc: VNC — weak password encryption, must not be exposed externally
note.network.port.redis: Redis — no default auth, access must be restricted
note.network.port.mongodb: MongoDB — major past leaks from unauthenticated exposure
progress.web.no_probes: No probes applicable for this depth
error.wifi.platform_not_supported: Wi-Fi scan is only supported on Windows (netsh wlan command). Linux/macOS support will come in v2.
error.wifi.netsh_failed: netsh wlan command failed — Wi-Fi adapter may be off or the command was not found. Make sure Windows Wi-Fi is enabled.
progress.wifi.no_networks: No Wi-Fi networks found nearby (netsh returned none)
finding.wifi.open.title: Unencrypted Wi-Fi network: {ssid}
finding.wifi.open.desc: Network `{ssid}` is broadcasting unencrypted. All non-HTTPS traffic of users connecting to this network can be eavesdropped by anyone nearby — passwords, cookies, form data. It is also open to MITM (Man-in-the-Middle) attacks. If this is your network, URGENTLY add a password + WPA2/WPA3.
finding.wifi.open.remediation: From your Wi-Fi router admin panel (usually 192.168.1.1), enable **WPA2-Personal** or **WPA3-Personal** encryption. Set a strong (12+ character, mixed) password. Hiding the SSID is NOT security — real protection is encryption + a strong password. For guests, create a separate guest Wi-Fi isolated from the internal network.
finding.wifi.wep.title: Wi-Fi network with WEP encryption: {ssid}
finding.wifi.wep.desc: Network `{ssid}` uses WEP encryption — an algorithm considered broken since 2007. A modern laptop (aircrack-ng) can recover the WEP key in minutes. This network should be treated as effectively unencrypted.
finding.wifi.wep.remediation: URGENT: From the router admin panel, change the encryption from WEP to **WPA2-Personal (AES/CCMP)** or **WPA3-Personal**. Refresh the WPA password as well. If legacy devices require WEP, move them to a separate network or replace them.
finding.wifi.oldwpa.title: Wi-Fi network with legacy WPA encryption: {ssid}
finding.wifi.oldwpa.desc: Network `{ssid}` uses original WPA (TKIP) — weaker than WPA2 and open to known attacks. Migrating to WPA2-AES is recommended.
finding.wifi.oldwpa.remediation: In the router, change encryption from WPA → **WPA2-Personal (AES/CCMP)**. If the router is not modern (pre-2015), consider replacing it — WPA3-capable models preferred.
finding.wifi.secure.title: Wi-Fi network detected: {ssid} ({auth})
finding.wifi.secure.desc: This network uses modern encryption ({auth}, {encryption}). It appears secure. Still, make sure you use a strong password and that your firmware is up to date.
finding.wifi.secure.remediation: Strong password + up-to-date router firmware + guest network isolation. If you are still on WPA2 and the router supports WPA3, switch to WPA3.
finding.redis.auth_open.title: Redis is accessible without a password — port {port}
finding.redis.auth_open.desc: Connected to the Redis server **without a password** (PING → PONG). In this configuration an attacker can read and delete all keys and, using `CONFIG SET dir /home/redis/.ssh` + `SAVE`, write SSH key files to take over the server (similar to `CVE-2022-0543`). This is one of the most common cloud-leak causes in practice.
finding.redis.auth_open.remediation: URGENT: In the Redis config (`/etc/redis/redis.conf`) set `requirepass <strong_password>` and `bind 127.0.0.1`. If remote access is required, restrict via firewall to specific IPs only. Prefer enabling TLS (`tls-port`). For Redis 6+ use ACL (`aclfile`).
finding.mongodb.auth_open.title: MongoDB is accessible without a password — port {port}
finding.mongodb.auth_open.desc: Connected to the MongoDB server **without a password** and retrieved the database list ({count} DBs visible). An attacker can read and delete all collections (this is how MongoDB ransomware attacks occur). Public-cloud open MongoDB instances have been the most common cause of data leaks since 2017.
finding.mongodb.auth_open.remediation: URGENT: Restart MongoDB with the `--auth` flag, create an admin user (`db.createUser({{...}})`). In `mongod.conf` set `security.authorization: enabled`. Also use `net.bindIp: 127.0.0.1` to bind only to localhost — if remote access is needed, restrict via firewall. Treat all existing DB contents as leaked.
finding.mysql.default_creds.title: MySQL accepts default credentials — {user}@{port}
finding.mysql.default_creds.desc: Connected to the MySQL server with `{user}:{password}` credentials (server version: {version}). An attacker can read all databases, destroy them via ALTER/DROP, or write files to the server via `SELECT ... INTO OUTFILE` (if write_privilege is enabled). Running MySQL in production with default credentials is a serious configuration error.
finding.mysql.default_creds.remediation: URGENT: Change the password with `ALTER USER 'root'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY '<strong_password>';`. If remote root access is not required, delete the remote root account with `DROP USER 'root'@'%';`. Set `bind-address = 127.0.0.1` to bind MySQL only to localhost. Run the `mysql_secure_installation` script (removes anonymous users, test DB).
finding.postgresql.default_creds.title: PostgreSQL accepts default credentials — {user}@{port}
finding.postgresql.default_creds.desc: Connected to the PostgreSQL server with `{user}:{password}` credentials (server: {version}). An attacker can read all schemas, cause data loss via DROP/ALTER, or execute commands on the server via extensions (`COPY ... TO PROGRAM`). Running production PostgreSQL with default credentials is a critical configuration error.
finding.postgresql.default_creds.remedia: URGENT: Change the password with `ALTER USER postgres WITH PASSWORD '<strong_password>';`. In `pg_hba.conf` require the `md5` or `scram-sha-256` auth method for remote connections. In `postgresql.conf` set `listen_addresses = 'localhost'` to bind only to localhost — if remote access is needed, allow specific IPs via firewall.
finding.ssh.default_creds.title: SSH accepts default credentials — {user}@{port}
finding.ssh.default_creds.desc: Connected to the SSH server with `{user}:{password}` (banner: {banner}). An attacker can open a shell, execute commands, read system information, and move laterally to adjacent systems. SSH brute-force is one of the most common attacks on the internet; running SSH with default credentials means full compromise of the server.
finding.ssh.default_creds.remediation: URGENT: Change the user's password with `passwd {user}` to a strong (12+ character, mixed) one. Better: **disable password-based SSH login entirely**, use SSH keys only (`PasswordAuthentication no` in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`). Also disable root login (`PermitRootLogin no`), install `fail2ban`, and change the SSH port (22 → 22xxx) to reduce automated scan surface.
finding.elasticsearch.auth_open.title: Elasticsearch is accessible without a password — port {port}
finding.elasticsearch.auth_open.desc: The Elasticsearch cluster root endpoint (`/`) responds without a password. In this configuration an attacker can pull all index data via `_search`, list tables via `_cat/indices`, and delete data. Since 2017, thousands of data leaks have been caused by such exposed ES instances.
finding.elasticsearch.auth_open.remediat: URGENT: In `elasticsearch.yml` set `xpack.security.enabled: true` and generate passwords with `elasticsearch-setup-passwords auto`. If only used internally, bind only to localhost via `network.host: 127.0.0.1`. In cloud environments, add firewall rules that allow port 9200 only from specific IPs.
finding.elasticsearch.auth_open.evidence: HTTP 200 + Elasticsearch cluster info returned (no auth)
finding.web.hsts_missing.title: HSTS missing
finding.web.hsts_missing.desc: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header is missing from the response. Without this header the browser is exposed to HTTPS→HTTP downgrade attacks. Recommended value: `max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains` (only on HTTPS sites).
finding.web.hsts_missing.remediation: Add to your server configuration: `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains`
finding.web.csp_missing.title: CSP missing
finding.web.csp_missing.desc: The Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header is missing. CSP is the most effective browser-level defense against XSS attacks. Starter value: `default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'`
finding.web.csp_missing.remediation: Start in report-only mode first: `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only: default-src 'self'`, then switch to enforced CSP.
finding.web.xfo_missing.title: X-Frame-Options missing (clickjacking protection)
finding.web.xfo_missing.desc: The X-Frame-Options header is missing. Without it, the page can be embedded via iframe on another site and used in clickjacking attacks. Recommended: `DENY` or `SAMEORIGIN`.
finding.web.xfo_missing.remediation: Add: `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN` (or the modern CSP equivalent `frame-ancestors 'self'`)
finding.web.xcto_missing.title: X-Content-Type-Options missing (MIME sniffing)
finding.web.xcto_missing.desc: The X-Content-Type-Options header is missing. Set it to `nosniff` to prevent browsers from guessing the content type.
finding.web.referrer_policy_missing.desc: The Referrer-Policy header is missing. The user's originating page information leaks to other sites. Recommended: `strict-origin-when-cross-origin`.
finding.web.version_leak.title: Version leak: {header}
finding.web.version_leak.desc: The server returns the `{header}: {value}` header in its response. An attacker can learn the target software version and try CVEs specific to it. Hiding this header does not add defense, but it does reduce the reconnaissance surface.
finding.web.version_leak.remediation: Remove the `{header}` header in your web server configuration. Nginx: `server_tokens off;` · Apache: `ServerTokens Prod`
finding.web.http_not_https.title: Served over HTTP (unencrypted)
finding.web.http_not_https.desc: The site is served over HTTP. All traffic (passwords, cookies, form data) can be read by attackers on the network. Modern browsers now flag HTTP sites as 'Not Secure'.
finding.web.http_not_https.remediation: Obtain a certificate via Let's Encrypt (free), set up HTTPS redirection on the server (301 redirect), and add an HSTS header.
finding.web.header_connection_failed.des: Could not connect to the site: {error}
finding.web.old_tls.title: Old TLS version supported: {proto}
finding.web.old_tls.desc: The server accepted a TLS handshake with the {proto} protocol. This version is outdated and exposed to known vulnerabilities (e.g. POODLE, BEAST). Modern browsers no longer support these versions.
finding.web.old_tls.remediation: Disable {proto} support in your server's TLS configuration. Only TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 should be enabled. Nginx: `ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;` · Apache: `SSLProtocol -all +TLSv1.2 +TLSv1.3`
finding.web.ssl_cert_error.title: SSL certificate issue
finding.web.ssl_cert_error.desc: Certificate validation failed: {reason}. This appears as a security warning in browsers and undermines user trust. Common causes: expired certificate, wrong hostname, self-signed certificate, missing chain certificate.
finding.web.ssl_cert_error.remediation: Install a valid SSL certificate. Free: Let's Encrypt (automated renewal via certbot). Commercial: reputable CAs such as DigiCert/Sectigo. Install the full certificate chain (including intermediates).
finding.web.path_traversal.title: Path traversal: `{param}` parameter
finding.web.path_traversal.desc: The `{param}` parameter does not prevent escaping the directory. With the {payload} payload, sensitive file contents on a {os_hint} system (`{matched_sig}`) appeared in the response. An attacker can reach critical data like `/etc/shadow`, application source code, or configuration files.
finding.web.path_traversal.remediation: Validate input for the `{param}` parameter: reject `..`, `/`, `\` characters; allow only filenames from an allowlist; use `os.path.realpath()` and verify the resolved path stays within the allowed root.
finding.web.sql_injection.title: SQL Injection: `{param}` parameter ({dbms})
finding.web.sql_injection.desc: When `{payload}` was sent in the `{param}` parameter, a {dbms} database error message was detected in the response body (`{matched_pattern}`). This indicates the parameter is being concatenated into the SQL query as a raw string rather than using prepared statements. An attacker can exploit this parameter for authentication bypass, database read, or (depending on privileges) modification.
finding.web.sql_injection.remediation: Use **prepared statements / parameterized queries** for this parameter in the database query. Instead of string concatenation, use `?` placeholders with bound variables. If using an ORM, prefer ORM methods over raw SQL. Temporary workaround: add a WAF (Cloudflare, ModSecurity) — but this is only temporary; parameterized queries at code level are mandatory.
finding.web.exposed_env.title: .env file publicly accessible
finding.web.exposed_env.desc: The `.env` file is accessible from the web root. This file typically contains database passwords, API keys, and secret keys.
finding.web.exposed_env.remediation: The `.env` file must never be in the web root — keep it one level above the app. Block access in the server and CHANGE all passwords/keys that were in the file.
finding.web.exposed_git_config.title: .git repository publicly accessible
finding.web.exposed_git_config.desc: The `.git/config` file is public. An attacker can extract all source code and hidden file history (via `git-dumper`).
finding.web.exposed_git_config.remediati: Remove the `.git/` directory completely from the web directory. Exclude `.git` during deployment. Nginx: `location ~ /\.git { deny all; }`
finding.web.exposed_git_head.title: .git/HEAD public
finding.web.exposed_git_head.desc: `.git/HEAD` is public — vulnerable to source code extraction.
finding.web.exposed_git_head.remediation: Remove the `.git/` directory completely from the web directory.
finding.web.exposed_backup_sql.title: Database backup public (backup.sql)
finding.web.exposed_backup_sql.desc: `/backup.sql` is accessible. The entire DB schema + data may leak.
finding.web.exposed_backup_sql.remediati: Never keep backup files in the web directory. Delete immediately and change DB passwords.
finding.web.exposed_database_sql.title: Database backup public (database.sql)
finding.web.exposed_database_sql.desc: `/database.sql` is accessible. DB content may leak.
finding.web.exposed_database_sql.remedia: Never keep backup files in the web directory. Delete immediately and change DB passwords.
finding.web.exposed_dump_sql.title: Database dump public (dump.sql)
finding.web.exposed_dump_sql.desc: `/dump.sql` is accessible. DB content may leak.
finding.web.exposed_dump_sql.remediation: Delete backup files and change DB passwords.
finding.web.exposed_wp_config_bak.title: WordPress configuration backup (.bak) leaked
finding.web.exposed_wp_config_bak.desc: `wp-config.php.bak` is accessible. Contains DB password and secret keys.
finding.web.exposed_wp_config_bak.remedi: Delete backups and change the WordPress DB password + secret keys.
finding.web.exposed_wp_config_save.title: WordPress configuration backup (.save) leaked
finding.web.exposed_wp_config_save.remed: Delete backups and change the WordPress DB password + secret keys.
finding.web.exposed_config_json.title: config.json public
finding.web.exposed_config_json.desc: Application configuration is exposed — may contain secrets.
finding.web.exposed_config_json.remediat: Do not keep configuration files in the web directory.
finding.web.exposed_config_yml.title: config.yml public
finding.web.exposed_config_yml.desc: Application configuration is exposed — may contain secrets.
finding.web.exposed_config_yml.remediati: Do not keep configuration files in the web directory.
finding.web.exposed_htaccess.title: .htaccess file accessible
finding.web.exposed_htaccess.desc: `.htaccess` Apache configuration should normally not be readable.
finding.web.exposed_htaccess.remediation: Fix server configuration — `.htaccess` must not be externally accessible.
finding.web.exposed_ds_store.title: .DS_Store leaked (macOS metadata)
finding.web.exposed_ds_store.desc: The `.DS_Store` file contains the list of files in the folder — leaks hidden file names to attackers.
finding.web.exposed_server_status.title: Apache server-status public
finding.web.exposed_server_status.desc: The `/server-status` page exposes active connections, requests, and vhost information.
finding.web.exposed_server_status.remedi: Disable the Apache `mod_status` module, or allow only localhost.
finding.web.exposed_phpinfo.title: phpinfo.php public
finding.web.exposed_phpinfo.desc: `phpinfo.php` is accessible. PHP configuration, paths, and environment variables are leaked.
finding.web.exposed_phpinfo.remediation: Delete `phpinfo.php` — development tools must not be in production.
finding.web.exposed_admin.title: Admin panel detected
finding.web.exposed_admin.desc: The `/admin` path points to a real admin panel. Not a vulnerability by itself, but it becomes a focus for attackers; strong authentication + IP restriction is recommended.
finding.web.exposed_admin.remediation: Allow access to the admin panel only via VPN or specific IPs. Strong password + MFA are mandatory.
finding.web.exposed_phpmyadmin.title: phpMyAdmin public
finding.web.exposed_phpmyadmin.desc: `/phpmyadmin` is accessible and a phpMyAdmin signature was detected. Becomes the first target of brute-force + exploit attempts.
finding.web.exposed_phpmyadmin.remediati: Uninstall phpMyAdmin or move it behind a VPN.
finding.web.security_txt_missing.title: security.txt missing
finding.web.security_txt_missing.desc: `/.well-known/security.txt` is missing. Publish this file so security researchers can report vulnerabilities to you.
finding.web.security_txt_missing.remedia: Create a `/.well-known/security.txt` file. Example contents: `Contact: mailto:security@example.com`, `Expires: 2027-01-01T00:00:00Z`. Details: https://securitytxt.org
evidence.web.exposed_paths.validator_mat: Content validator matched — real file signature detected
evidence.web.exposed_paths.not_found: File not found or not accessible
finding.web.xss.title: Reflected XSS: `{param}` parameter
finding.web.xss.desc: An HTML/JS payload sent in the `{param}` parameter was reflected back unescaped into the response. This means an attacker could craft a specially-formed link that, when clicked by a user, executes JavaScript in the user's browser (session cookie theft, phishing, DOM manipulation). Triggering payload: `{payload}`
finding.web.xss.remediation: Apply **context-aware escaping** before writing user input to HTML: HTML body → `html_escape`, attribute → `attr_escape`, JS string → `js_escape`. In modern frameworks (React, Vue, Jinja2 autoescape) this is the default — `{{ variable }}` auto-escapes, `{{ variable | safe }}` is dangerous. As additional defense, add a CSP header: `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'`
finding.web.xss_echo_fallback.title: Site reflects any parameter — XSS test skipped
finding.web.xss_echo_fallback.desc: This site responds by reflecting even a randomly-named parameter's content (SPA fallback, dev server echo, debug endpoint, etc.). Classic reflected XSS tests yield false positives on such sites. **If this is a production system**, review the input echoing behavior with your developer team — if HTML escaping is not applied, there IS a real XSS vulnerability.
finding.web.xss_echo_fallback.remediatio: For developers: apply HTML escaping before rendering user input in every response. If you use a framework (React/Vue/Jinja2), make sure autoescape is active.
finding.web.xss_threshold_exceeded.title: Site reflects most parameters — XSS test not reliable
finding.web.xss_threshold_exceeded.desc: {reflected_count} of {tested_count} tested parameters were detected as 'reflected'. In the real world it is rare for this many parameters to all have XSS — most likely the site reflects every parameter input into its response (SPA fallback, debug endpoint, dev server echo). Classic reflected XSS tests yield false positives on such sites. Check with the developer team to verify HTML escape behavior.
finding.web.xss_threshold_exceeded.remed: For developers: apply context-aware escaping before writing user input to HTML. Framework (React/Vue/Jinja2) autoescape should be on by default.
